question:
7. Let \( g(t) \) and \( h(t) \) be real, continuous functions for \( t \geq 0 \). Show that any function \( v(t) \) satisfying the differential inequality
\[
\frac{d v}{d t}+g(t) v \geq h(t), \quad v(0)=c
\]
satisfies the further inequality \( v(t) \geq u(t) \) where
\[
\frac{d u}{d t}+g(t) u=h(t), \quad u(0)=c
\]

From this, conclude that for sufficiently small \( t>0 \), the solution of
\[
\frac{d v}{d t}+g(t) v=v^{2}, \quad v(0)=c_{1}
\]
may be written
\[
v=\max _{w}\left[c_{1} e^{-\int_{0}^{t}[g(s)-2 w(s)] d s}-\int_{0}^{t} e^{-\int_{0}^{t}\left[g\left(s_{1}\right)-2 w\left(s_{1}\right)\right] d s_{1}} w^{2}(s) d s\right]
\]
where the maximization is over all continuous functions \( \boldsymbol{w}(t) \) defined over some \( t \)-interval \( \left[0, t_{0}\right] \).

descriptive_long.question:
Let \( coeffg(timevar) \) and \( inhomfn(timevar) \) be real, continuous functions for \( timevar \geq 0 \). Show that any function \( statefn(timevar) \) satisfying the differential inequality
\[
\frac{d statefn}{d timevar}+coeffg(timevar) statefn \geq inhomfn(timevar), \quad statefn(0)=initcst
\]
satisfies the further inequality \( statefn(timevar) \geq auxfunc(timevar) \) where
\[
\frac{d auxfunc}{d timevar}+coeffg(timevar) auxfunc=inhomfn(timevar), \quad auxfunc(0)=initcst
\]

From this, conclude that for sufficiently small \( timevar>0 \), the solution of
\[
\frac{d statefn}{d timevar}+coeffg(timevar) statefn=statefn^{2}, \quad statefn(0)=initone
\]
may be written
\[
statefn=\max _{trialfn}\left[initone e^{-\int_{0}^{timevar}[coeffg(sample)-2 trialfn(sample)] d sample}-\int_{0}^{timevar} e^{-\int_{0}^{timevar}\left[coeffg\left(sampleone\right)-2 trialfn\left(sampleone\right)\right] d sampleone} trialfn^{2}(sample) d sample\right]
\]
where the maximization is over all continuous functions \( \boldsymbol{trialfn}(timevar) \) defined over some \( timevar \)-interval \( \left[0, boundzero\right] \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
accumg(timevar)=\int_{0}^{timevar} coeffg(sample) d sample .
\]

Then \( accumg^{\prime}=coeffg \). Put \( transvar=auxfunc e^{accumg}, \; transyvar=statefn e^{accumg} \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d transvar}{d timevar}=\left(\frac{d auxfunc}{d timevar}+coeffg auxfunc\right) e^{accumg}=inhomfn e^{accumg} \\
\frac{d transyvar}{d timevar}=\left(\frac{d statefn}{d timevar}+coeffg statefn\right) e^{accumg} \geq inhomfn e^{accumg}
\end{array}
\]
since \( e^{accumg}>0 \) for all \( timevar \). Thus \( (d / d timevar)(transyvar-transvar) \geq 0 \). Now \( transvar(0)=transyvar(0)=initcst \), and we conclude \( transvar(timevar) \leq transyvar(timevar) \) and therefore \( auxfunc(timevar) \leq statefn(timevar) \) for all \( timevar \geq 0 \).

For the second part of the problem, we suppose that \( statefn \) satisfies
\[
\frac{d statefn}{d timevar}+coeffg statefn=statefn^{2} \text { for } timevar \in\left[0, boundzero\right], \quad statefn(0)=initone
\]
and we let \( trialfn \) be any continuous function on \( \left[0, boundzero\right] \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
(statefn-trialfn)^{2} \geq 0 \quad \text { for all } timevar \\
\frac{d statefn}{d timevar}+(coeffg-2 trialfn) statefn=statefn^{2}-2 trialfn statefn \geq-trialfn^{2}
\end{array}
\]

If \( accumw(timevar)=\int_{0}^{1} trialfn(sample) d sample \) and \( transyvar=statefn e^{accumg-2 accumw} \) then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d transyvar}{d timevar}=\left(\frac{d statefn}{d timevar}+(coeffg-2 trialfn) statefn\right) e^{accumg-2 accumw} \geq-trialfn^{2} e^{accumg-2 accumw} \\
transyvar(timevar) \geq initone-\int_{0}^{\prime} trialfn^{2}(sample) \exp [accumg(sample)-2 accumw(sample)] d sample \\
statefn(timevar) \geq initone \exp [-accumg(timevar)+2 accumw(timevar)] \\
-\int_{0}^{\prime} \exp [accumg(sample)-accumg(timevar)-2(accumw(sample)-accumw(timevar))] trialfn^{2}(sample) d sample
\end{array}
\]

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
statefn(timevar) \geq \max _{trialfn}\{ & \left\{initone \exp [-accumg(timevar)+2 accumw(timevar)]\right. \\
& \left.-\int_{0}^{\prime \prime} \exp [accumg(sample)-accumg(timevar)-2(accumw(sample)-accumw(timevar))] trialfn^{2}(sample) d sample\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]

Now if we take \( trialfn=statefn \), then all of the preceding inequalities become equalities so we conclude
\[
\begin{aligned}
statefn(timevar)=\max _{n^{\prime}}\{ & initone \exp [-accumg(timevar)+2 accumw(timevar)] \\
& \left.-\int_{0}^{timevar} \exp [accumg(sample)-accumg(timevar)-2(accumw(sample)-accumw(timevar))] trialfn^{2}(sample) d sample\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is equivalent to the form given.

The solution of the equation
\[
\frac{d statefn}{d timevar}+coeffg statefn=statefn^{2}
\]
subject to the initial condition \( statefn(0)=initone \), may be unbounded on a finite interval, say \( statefn \rightarrow \infty \) as \( timevar \mid boundone \). Then the preceding analysis is valid only for \( timevar<boundone \). If we pick any \( boundzero \) with \( 0<boundzero<boundone \), then it is valid for [0, \( \left.boundzero\right] \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
7. Let \( mapleroot(lantern) \) and \( windwhisper(lantern) \) be real, continuous functions for \( lantern \geq 0 \). Show that any function \( orchardbird(lantern) \) satisfying the differential inequality
\[\frac{d orchardbird}{d lantern}+mapleroot(lantern)\,orchardbird(lantern) \geq windwhisper(lantern), \quad orchardbird(0)=rainshadow\]
satisfies the further inequality \( orchardbird(lantern) \geq riverstone(lantern) \) where
\[\frac{d riverstone}{d lantern}+mapleroot(lantern)\,riverstone(lantern)=windwhisper(lantern), \quad riverstone(0)=rainshadow\]

From this, conclude that for sufficiently small \( lantern>0 \), the solution of
\[\frac{d orchardbird}{d lantern}+mapleroot(lantern)\,orchardbird(lantern)=orchardbird^{2}, \quad orchardbird(0)=stairwell\]
may be written
\[orchardbird=\max _{pinecone}\left[stairwell\,e^{-\int_{0}^{lantern}[mapleroot(meadowbrook)-2\,pinecone(meadowbrook)]\,d meadowbrook}-\int_{0}^{lantern}e^{-\int_{0}^{lantern}\left[mapleroot\left(cottonseed\right)-2\,pinecone\left(cottonseed\right)\right]d cottonseed}\,pinecone^{2}(meadowbrook)\,d meadowbrook\right]\]
where the maximization is over all continuous functions \( \boldsymbol{pinecone}(lantern) \) defined over some \( lantern \)-interval \( \left[0, moondance\right] \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let
\[foxglove(lantern)=\int_{0}^{lantern} mapleroot(meadowbrook)\,d meadowbrook .\]

Then \( foxglove^{\prime}=mapleroot \). Put \( ironbridge=riverstone e^{foxglove},\; silvercloud=orchardbird e^{foxglove} \). Then
\[\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d ironbridge}{d lantern}=\left(\frac{d riverstone}{d lantern}+mapleroot\,riverstone\right)e^{foxglove}=windwhisper e^{foxglove}\\
\frac{d silvercloud}{d lantern}=\left(\frac{d orchardbird}{d lantern}+mapleroot\,orchardbird\right)e^{foxglove}\geq windwhisper e^{foxglove}
\end{array}\]
since \( e^{foxglove}>0 \) for all \( lantern \). Thus \( (d/ d lantern)(silvercloud-ironbridge)\geq0 \). Now \( ironbridge(0)=silvercloud(0)=rainshadow \), and we conclude \( ironbridge(lantern)\leq silvercloud(lantern) \) and therefore \( riverstone(lantern)\leq orchardbird(lantern) \) for all \( lantern\geq0 \).

For the second part of the problem, we suppose that \( orchardbird \) satisfies
\[\frac{d orchardbird}{d lantern}+mapleroot\,orchardbird=orchardbird^{2}\text{ for } lantern\in[0,moondance],\quad orchardbird(0)=stairwell\]
and we let \( pinecone \) be any continuous function on \([0,moondance]\). Then
\[\begin{array}{c}
(orchardbird-pinecone)^{2}\geq0\quad\text{ for all } lantern\\
\frac{d orchardbird}{d lantern}+(mapleroot-2 pinecone)\,orchardbird=orchardbird^{2}-2 pinecone\,orchardbird\geq-pinecone^{2}
\end{array}\]

If \( hummingway(lantern)=\int_{0}^{1} pinecone(meadowbrook)\,d meadowbrook \) and \( silvercloud=orchardbird\,e^{foxglove 2 pinecone} \) then
\[\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d silvercloud}{d lantern}=\left(\frac{d orchardbird}{d lantern}+(mapleroot-2 pinecone)\,orchardbird\right)e^{foxglove-2 pinecone}\geq-pinecone^{2}e^{foxglove}2 pinecone\\
silvercloud(lantern)\geq stairwell-\int_{0}^{\prime} pinecone^{2}(meadowbrook)\exp[foxglove(meadowbrook)-2 hummingway(meadowbrook)]\,d meadowbrook\\
orchardbird(lantern)\geq stairwell\exp[-foxglove(lantern)+2 hummingway(lantern)]\\
-\int_{0}^{\prime} \exp[foxglove(meadowbrook)-foxglove(lantern)-2(hummingway(meadowbrook)-hummingway(lantern))] pinecone^{2}(meadowbrook)\,d meadowbrook
\end{array}\]

So
\[\begin{aligned}
orchardbird(lantern)\geq \max_{pinecone}\{&\,stairwell\exp[-foxglove(lantern)+2 hummingway(lantern)]\\
&-\int_{0}^{\prime\prime}\exp[foxglove(meadowbrook)-foxglove(lantern)-2(hummingway(meadowbrook)-hummingway(lantern))] pinecone^{2}(meadowbrook)\,d meadowbrook\}
\end{aligned}\]

Now if we take \( pinecone=orchardbird \), then all of the preceding inequalities become equalities so we conclude
\[\begin{aligned}
orchardbird(lantern)=\max_{n^{\prime}}\{&\,stairwell\exp[-foxglove(lantern)+2 hummingway(lantern)]\\
&-\int_{0}^{lantern}\exp[foxglove(meadowbrook)-foxglove(lantern)-2(hummingway(meadowbrook)-hummingway(lantern))] pinecone^{2}(meadowbrook)\,d meadowbrook\}
\end{aligned}\]
which is equivalent to the form given.

The solution of the equation
\[\frac{d orchardbird}{d lantern}+mapleroot\,orchardbird=orchardbird^{2}\]
subject to the initial condition \( orchardbird(0)=stairwell \), may be unbounded on a finite interval, say \( orchardbird\to\infty \) as \( lantern\mid amberlight \). Then the preceding analysis is valid only for \( lantern<amberlight \). If we pick any \( moondance \) with \( 0<moondance<amberlight \), then it is valid for [0, \( \left.moondance\right] \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
7. Let \( levityfun(spacedist) \) and \( unrestfun(spacedist) \) be real, continuous functions for \( spacedist \geq 0 \). Show that any function \( reststate(spacedist) \) satisfying the differential inequality
\[
\frac{d reststate}{d spacedist}+levityfun(spacedist) reststate \geq unrestfun(spacedist), \quad reststate(0)=variable
\]
satisfies the further inequality \( reststate(spacedist) \geq chaosfunc(spacedist) \) where
\[
\frac{d chaosfunc}{d spacedist}+levityfun(spacedist) chaosfunc=unrestfun(spacedist), \quad chaosfunc(0)=variable
\]

From this, conclude that for sufficiently small \( spacedist>0 \), the solution of
\[
\frac{d reststate}{d spacedist}+levityfun(spacedist) reststate=reststate^{2}, \quad reststate(0)=volatile
\]
may be written
\[
reststate=\max _{steadfun}\left[volatile e^{-\int_{0}^{spacedist}[levityfun(stillness)-2 steadfun(stillness)] d stillness}-\int_{0}^{spacedist} e^{-\int_{0}^{spacedist}\left[levityfun\left(stillnessone\right)-2 steadfun\left(stillnessone\right)\right] d stillnessone} steadfun^{2}(stillness) d stillness\right]
\]
where the maximization is over all continuous functions \( \boldsymbol{steadfun}(spacedist) \) defined over some \( spacedist \)-interval \( \left[0, endlesses\right] \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
disintegral(spacedist)=\int_{0}^{spacedist} levityfun(stillness) d stillness .
\]

Then \( disintegral^{\prime}=levityfun \). Put \( depthval=chaosfunc e^{disintegral}, sidevalue=reststate e^{disintegral} \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d depthval}{d spacedist}=\left(\frac{d chaosfunc}{d spacedist}+levityfun chaosfunc\right) e^{disintegral}=unrestfun e^{disintegral} \\
\frac{d sidevalue}{d spacedist}=\left(\frac{d reststate}{d spacedist}+levityfun reststate\right) e^{disintegral} \geq unrestfun e^{disintegral}
\end{array}
\]
since \( e^{disintegral}>0 \) for all \( spacedist \). Thus \( (d / d spacedist)(sidevalue-depthval) \geq 0 \). Now \( depthval(0)=sidevalue(0)=variable \), and we conclude \( depthval(spacedist) \leq sidevalue(spacedist) \) and therefore \( chaosfunc(spacedist) \leq reststate(spacedist) \) for all \( spacedist \geq 0 \).

For the second part of the problem, we suppose that \( reststate \) satisfies
\[
\frac{d reststate}{d spacedist}+levityfun reststate=reststate^{2} \text { for } spacedist \in\left[0, endlesses\right], \quad reststate(0)=volatile
\]
and we let \( steadfun \) be any continuous function on \( \left[0, endlesses\right] \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
(reststate-steadfun)^{2} \geq 0 \quad \text { for all } spacedist \\
\frac{d reststate}{d spacedist}+(levityfun-2 steadfun) reststate=reststate^{2}-2 steadfun reststate \geq-steadfun^{2}
\end{array}
\]

If \( emptiness(spacedist)=\int_{0}^{1} steadfun(stillness) d stillness \) and \( sidevalue=reststate e^{disintegral 2 steadfun} \) then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d sidevalue}{d spacedist}=\left(\frac{d reststate}{d spacedist}+(levityfun-2 steadfun) reststate\right) e^{disintegral-2 steadfun} \geq-steadfun^{2} e^{disintegral} 2 steadfun \\
sidevalue(spacedist) \geq volatile-\int_{0}^{\prime} steadfun^{2}(stillness) \exp [disintegral(stillness)-2 emptiness(stillness)] d stillness \\
reststate(spacedist) \geq volatile \exp [-disintegral(spacedist)+2 emptiness(spacedist)] \\
-\int_{0}^{\prime} \exp [disintegral(stillness)-disintegral(spacedist)-2(emptiness(stillness)-emptiness(spacedist))] steadfun^{2}(stillness) d stillness
\end{array}
\]

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
reststate(spacedist) \geq \max _{steadfun}\{ & \left\{volatile \exp [-disintegral(spacedist)+2 emptiness(spacedist)]\right. \\
& \left.-\int_{0}^{\prime \prime} \exp [disintegral(stillness)-disintegral(spacedist)-2(emptiness(stillness)-emptiness(spacedist))] steadfun^{2}(stillness) d stillness\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]

Now if we take \( steadfun=reststate \), then all of the preceding inequalities become equalities so we conclude
\[
\begin{aligned}
reststate(spacedist)=\max _{n^{\prime}}\{ & volatile \exp [-disintegral(spacedist)+2 emptiness(spacedist)] \\
& \left.-\int_{0}^{spacedist} \exp [disintegral(stillness)-disintegral(spacedist)-2(emptiness(stillness)-emptiness(spacedist))] steadfun^{2}(stillness) d stillness\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is equivalent to the form given.
The solution of the equation
\[
\frac{d reststate}{d spacedist}+levityfun reststate=reststate^{2}
\]
subject to the initial condition \( reststate(0)=volatile \), may be unbounded on a finite interval, say \( reststate \rightarrow \infty \) as \( spacedist \mid eternity \). Then the preceding analysis is valid only for \( spacedist<eternity \). If we pick any \( endlesses \) with \( 0<endlesses<eternity \), then it is valid for [0, \( \left.endlesses\right] \).

garbled_string.question:
7. Let \( szepidra(akpsejrm) \) and \( qlonfuvk(akpsejrm) \) be real, continuous functions for \( akpsejrm \geq 0 \). Show that any function \( plxmoqns(akpsejrm) \) satisfying the differential inequality
\[
\frac{d plxmoqns}{d akpsejrm}+szepidra(akpsejrm) plxmoqns \geq qlonfuvk(akpsejrm), \quad plxmoqns(0)=wesdjnol
\]
satisfies the further inequality \( plxmoqns(akpsejrm) \geq ykgrtabe(akpsejrm) \) where
\[
\frac{d ykgrtabe}{d akpsejrm}+szepidra(akpsejrm) ykgrtabe=qlonfuvk(akpsejrm), \quad ykgrtabe(0)=wesdjnol
\]

From this, conclude that for sufficiently small \( akpsejrm>0 \), the solution of
\[
\frac{d plxmoqns}{d akpsejrm}+szepidra(akpsejrm) plxmoqns=plxmoqns^{2}, \quad plxmoqns(0)=ytmkzabe
\]
may be written
\[
plxmoqns=\max _{dzlhvsep}\left[ytmkzabe e^{-\int_{0}^{akpsejrm}[szepidra(fnduqzlo)-2 dzlhvsep(fnduqzlo)] d fnduqzlo}-\int_{0}^{akpsejrm} e^{-\int_{0}^{akpsejrm}\left[szepidra\left(htrivcpm\right)-2 dzlhvsep\left(htrivcpm\right)\right] d htrivcpm} dzlhvsep^{2}(fnduqzlo) d fnduqzlo\right]
\]
where the maximization is over all continuous functions \( \boldsymbol{dzlhvsep}(akpsejrm) \) defined over some \( akpsejrm \)-interval \( \left[0, kxbrtahu\right] \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
bvcayzth(akpsejrm)=\int_{0}^{akpsejrm} szepidra(fnduqzlo) d fnduqzlo .
\]

Then \( bvcayzth^{\prime}=szepidra \). Put \( jbqnarud=ykgrtabe e^{bvcayzth}, kigwesom=plxmoqns e^{bvcayzth} \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d jbqnarud}{d akpsejrm}=\left(\frac{d ykgrtabe}{d akpsejrm}+szepidra ykgrtabe\right) e^{bvcayzth}=qlonfuvk e^{bvcayzth} \\
\frac{d kigwesom}{d akpsejrm}=\left(\frac{d plxmoqns}{d akpsejrm}+szepidra plxmoqns\right) e^{bvcayzth} \geq qlonfuvk e^{bvcayzth}
\end{array}
\]
since \( e^{bvcayzth}>0 \) for all \( akpsejrm \). Thus \( (d / d akpsejrm)(kigwesom-jbqnarud) \geq 0 \). Now \( jbqnarud(0)=kigwesom(0)=wesdjnol \), and we conclude \( jbqnarud(akpsejrm) \leq kigwesom(akpsejrm) \) and therefore \( ykgrtabe(akpsejrm) \leq plxmoqns(akpsejrm) \) for all \( akpsejrm \geq 0 \).

For the second part of the problem, we suppose that \( plxmoqns \) satisfies
\[
\frac{d plxmoqns}{d akpsejrm}+szepidra plxmoqns=plxmoqns^{2} \text { for } akpsejrm \in\left[0, kxbrtahu\right], \quad plxmoqns(0)=ytmkzabe
\]
and we let \( dzlhvsep \) be any continuous function on \( \left[0, kxbrtahu\right] \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
(plxmoqns-dzlhvsep)^{2} \geq 0 \quad \text { for all } akpsejrm \\
\frac{d plxmoqns}{d akpsejrm}+(szepidra-2 dzlhvsep) plxmoqns=plxmoqns^{2}-2 dzlhvsep plxmoqns \geq-dzlhvsep^{2}
\end{array}
\]

If \( mljxprwa(akpsejrm)=\int_{0}^{1} dzlhvsep(fnduqzlo) d fnduqzlo \) and \( kigwesom=plxmoqns e^{bvcayzth 2 dzlhvsep} \) then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d kigwesom}{d akpsejrm}=\left(\frac{d plxmoqns}{d akpsejrm}+(szepidra-2 dzlhvsep) plxmoqns\right) e^{bvcayzth-2 dzlhvsep} \geq-dzlhvsep^{2} e^{bvcayzth} 2 dzlhvsep \\
kigwesom(akpsejrm) \geq ytmkzabe-\int_{0}^{\prime} dzlhvsep^{2}(fnduqzlo) \exp [bvcayzth(fnduqzlo)-2 mljxprwa(fnduqzlo)] d fnduqzlo \\
plxmoqns(akpsejrm) \geq ytmkzabe \exp [-bvcayzth(akpsejrm)+2 mljxprwa(akpsejrm)] \\
-\int_{0}^{\prime} \exp [bvcayzth(fnduqzlo)-bvcayzth(akpsejrm)-2( mljxprwa(fnduqzlo)- mljxprwa(akpsejrm))] dzlhvsep^{2}(fnduqzlo) d fnduqzlo
\end{array}
\]

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
plxmoqns(akpsejrm) \geq \max _{dzlhvsep}\{ & \left\{ytmkzabe \exp [-bvcayzth(akpsejrm)+2 mljxprwa(akpsejrm)]\right. \\
& \left.-\int_{0}^{\prime \prime} \exp [bvcayzth(fnduqzlo)-bvcayzth(akpsejrm)-2( mljxprwa(fnduqzlo)- mljxprwa(akpsejrm))] dzlhvsep^{2}(fnduqzlo) d fnduqzlo\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]

Now if we take \( dzlhvsep=plxmoqns \), then all of the preceding inequalities become equalities so we conclude
\[
\begin{aligned}
plxmoqns(akpsejrm)=\max _{n^{\prime}}\{ & ytmkzabe \exp [-bvcayzth(akpsejrm)+2 mljxprwa(akpsejrm)] \\
& \left.-\int_{0}^{akpsejrm} \exp [bvcayzth(fnduqzlo)-bvcayzth(akpsejrm)-2( mljxprwa(fnduqzlo)- mljxprwa(akpsejrm))] dzlhvsep^{2}(fnduqzlo) d fnduqzlo\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is equivalent to the form given.
The solution of the equation
\[
\frac{d plxmoqns}{d akpsejrm}+szepidra plxmoqns=plxmoqns^{2}
\]
subject to the initial condition \( plxmoqns(0)=ytmkzabe \), may be unbounded on a finite interval, say \( plxmoqns \rightarrow \infty \) as \( akpsejrm \mid nsgpdewi \). Then the preceding analysis is valid only for \( akpsejrm<nsgpdewi \). If we pick any \( kxbrtahu \) with \( 0<kxbrtahu<nsgpdewi \), then it is valid for [0, \( \left.kxbrtahu\right] \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let n\in \mathbb{N} and a\in \mathbb{R}.  Denote by S^n the space of real symmetric n\times n matrices, partially ordered by the Loewner cone  
A \succeq  B  \Leftrightarrow   A-B is positive-semidefinite.  All matrix entries are Lebesgue-measurable and every integral below is a Lebesgue integral.

1.  (Matrix-valued linear comparison.)  
    Let  
       G:[a,\infty )\to \mathbb{R}^{n\times n},  H:[a,\infty )\to S^n  
    be locally integrable, and let V:[a,\infty )\to S^n be absolutely continuous and satisfy, for a.e. t\geq a,  
       V'(t)+G(t)^TV(t)+V(t)G(t) \succeq  H(t),  V(a)=S\in S^n.  
    Denote by U the unique absolutely continuous solution of the matrix ODE  
       U'(t)+G(t)^TU(t)+U(t)G(t)=H(t), U(a)=S.  
    Prove the comparison principle  
       V(t) \succeq  U(t) for every t\geq a.

2.  (Variational representation for a matrix Riccati equation.)  
    Fix \delta >0 and let  
       Q:[a,a+\delta )\to S^n  
    be locally integrable.  Suppose V:[a,a+\delta )\to S^n is absolutely continuous and solves the matrix Riccati equation  
       V'(t)+G(t)^TV(t)+V(t)G(t)=V(t)^2+Q(t), V(a)=S_0\in S^n.  (\star )  
    For any W\in L^2_loc([a,a+\delta );S^n) define \Phi _W:[a,a+\delta )\to \mathbb{R}^{n\times n} by  
       \Phi _W'(t)=(G(t)-W(t))\Phi _W(t), \Phi _W(a)=I_n.  (\dagger )

    Show that for every t\in [a,a+\delta )  
       V(t)= sup_{W\in L^2_loc} \Xi (t,W),                  (\ddagger )  
    where  
       \Xi (t,W)=\Phi _W(t)^{-^T} S_0 \Phi _W(t)^{-1}  
               -\int _a^{t} \Phi _W(t)^{-^T} \Phi _W(s)^T[W(s)^2-Q(s)]\Phi _W(s) \Phi _W(t)^{-1} ds.  

    (The supremum is taken with respect to the Loewner order on S^n; it is actually a maximum and is attained for the choice W=V.)

    You may assume \delta  is chosen so that V is finite on [a,a+\delta ).
kernel_variant.solution:
Part 1: Linear comparison in the Loewner order  
---------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  Fundamental matrix.  
Solve the auxiliary linear system  
   \Psi '(t)=G(t)\Psi (t), \Psi (a)=I_n.  
Local integrability of G guarantees an absolutely continuous, point-wise invertible \Psi  with \Psi ,\Psi ^{-1} locally absolutely continuous.

Step 2.  Conjugation.  
Put X(t):=\Psi (t)^T V(t) \Psi (t).  Because V and \Psi  are absolutely continuous, so is X.  Differentiating,

   X'(t)=\Psi (t)^T[ V'(t)+G(t)^TV(t)+V(t)G(t) ]\Psi (t).

Using the hypothesis V'+G^TV+VG \succeq  H,

   X'(t) \succeq  \Psi (t)^T H(t) \Psi (t).               (1)

Step 3.  Comparison with the linear solution.  
Define X_U(t):=\Psi (t)^T U(t) \Psi (t); since U satisfies the equality,

   X_U'(t)=\Psi (t)^T H(t) \Psi (t).               (2)

Set Y(t):=X(t)-X_U(t).  From (1)-(2) we have Y'(t) \succeq  0 a.e. and Y(a)=0, hence Y(t) \succeq  0 for all t\geq a.  Multiplying by \Psi (t)^{-^T} (left) and \Psi (t)^{-1} (right) preserves the Loewner order, giving V(t) \succeq  U(t).

Part 2: Variational representation for the Riccati solution  
----------------------------------------------------------------
Throughout let W\in L^2_loc([a,a+\delta );S^n) be arbitrary.

Step 1.  A quadratic matrix inequality.  
For symmetric matrices Z,W the square (Z-W)^2 is positive-semidefinite; expanding,

   (Z-W)^2 = Z^2 - ZW - WZ + W^2 \succeq  0  
   \Rightarrow  Z^2 \succeq  ZW + WZ - W^2.               (3)

Taking Z=V(t) (symmetric) in (3) we obtain

   V(t)^2 \succeq  V(t)W(t) + W(t)V(t) - W(t)^2.        (4)

Step 2.  Inserting (4) into the Riccati equation.  
Equation (\star ) and (4) give, for a.e. t,

   V' + G^TV + VG 
     = V^2 + Q
     \succeq  V W + W V - W^2 + Q.             (5)

Re-grouping the mixed terms,

   V' + (G-W)^TV + V(G-W) \succeq  Q - W^2.        (6)

Notice that (G-W)^TV+V(G-W) is symmetric because V and W are.

Step 3.  Conjugation by the flow \Phi _W.  
Let \Phi _W solve (\dagger ) and set  

   X_W(t):=\Phi _W(t)^T V(t) \Phi _W(t).           (7)

Because \Phi _W'=(G-W)\Phi _W and \Phi _W is invertible, differentiating (7) yields

   X_W'(t)=\Phi _W(t)^T
            [ V' + (G-W)^TV + V(G-W) ](t)
            \Phi _W(t)
          \succeq  \Phi _W(t)^T[ Q(t) - W(t)^2 ] \Phi _W(t).   (8)

Step 4.  Integration.  
Integrate (8) from a to t (\Phi _W(a)=I_n):

   X_W(t) \succeq  S_0 - \int _a^{t} \Phi _W(s)^T[ W(s)^2 - Q(s) ] \Phi _W(s) ds. (9)

Step 5.  Returning to V.  
Premultiplying (9) by \Phi _W(t)^{-^T} and post-multiplying by \Phi _W(t)^{-1} gives

   V(t) \succeq  \Phi _W(t)^{-^T}S_0\Phi _W(t)^{-1}
          -\int _a^{t} \Phi _W(t)^{-^T} \Phi _W(s)^T[ W(s)^2-Q(s) ]
                     \Phi _W(s) \Phi _W(t)^{-1} ds
        = \Xi (t,W).                 (10)

Hence V(t) dominates every \Xi (t,W); therefore

   V(t) \succeq  sup_{W\in L^2_loc} \Xi (t,W).          (11)

Step 6.  Equality for the maximising choice W=V.  
Choose W=V.  In (3) we then have equality, and consequently every ``\succeq '' in (5)-(10) becomes ``=''.  Thus \Xi (t,V)=V(t), so V(t) realises the supremum.  Therefore the supremum in (\ddagger ) is a maximum attained at W=V and (11) holds with equality:

   V(t)= sup_{W\in L^2_loc} \Xi (t,W)=\Xi (t,V).  

Step 7.  Well-posedness remarks.  
* For each W the flow \Phi _W exists and is invertible because G-W is locally integrable.  
* The integrand in \Xi (t,W) is locally square-integrable, hence the integral is finite on [a,t).  
* Although (S^n,\succeq ) is not a lattice when n\geq 2, the supremum in (\ddagger ) exists since it is achieved at W=V.

This completes the proof of (\ddagger ).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimensional structure: The scalar functions of the original problem are replaced by symmetric n×n matrices, forcing the use of the non-trivial Loewner partial order and properties of matrix positive–semidefiniteness.  
• Non-commutativity: Conjugation by a fundamental matrix and matrix products introduce non-commuting factors, greatly complicating differentiation and order preservation.  
• Riccati dynamics: The non-linear part is now matrix-quadratic (V²), the natural multivariate analogue of the scalar v², and requires careful handling via the inequality (Z−W)² ≽ 0.  
• Control–theoretic flavour: The representation involves an auxiliary matrix control W and a matrix differential equation (†) for Φ_W, mirroring advanced linear–quadratic optimal–control theory.  
• Cone-valued analysis: All arguments must respect the Loewner cone; standard scalar inequalities do not directly apply and have to be re-established for matrices.  
• Technical prerequisites: Knowledge of fundamental matrices, matrix ODEs, invariance of the cone under similarity transforms, and integration of operator-valued functions is required.  
Hence the enhanced variant is substantially more intricate and cannot be solved by a straightforward lift of the original scalar techniques.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n\in \mathbb{N} and a\in \mathbb{R}.  Denote by S^n the space of real symmetric n\times n matrices, partially ordered by the Loewner cone  
A \succeq  B  \Leftrightarrow   A-B is positive-semidefinite.  All matrix entries are Lebesgue-measurable and every integral below is a Lebesgue integral.

1.  (Matrix-valued linear comparison.)  
    Let  
       G:[a,\infty )\to \mathbb{R}^{n\times n},  H:[a,\infty )\to S^n  
    be locally integrable, and let V:[a,\infty )\to S^n be absolutely continuous and satisfy, for a.e. t\geq a,  
       V'(t)+G(t)^TV(t)+V(t)G(t) \succeq  H(t),  V(a)=S\in S^n.  
    Denote by U the unique absolutely continuous solution of the matrix ODE  
       U'(t)+G(t)^TU(t)+U(t)G(t)=H(t), U(a)=S.  
    Prove the comparison principle  
       V(t) \succeq  U(t) for every t\geq a.

2.  (Variational representation for a matrix Riccati equation.)  
    Fix \delta >0 and let  
       Q:[a,a+\delta )\to S^n  
    be locally integrable.  Suppose V:[a,a+\delta )\to S^n is absolutely continuous and solves the matrix Riccati equation  
       V'(t)+G(t)^TV(t)+V(t)G(t)=V(t)^2+Q(t), V(a)=S_0\in S^n.  (\star )  
    For any W\in L^2_loc([a,a+\delta );S^n) define \Phi _W:[a,a+\delta )\to \mathbb{R}^{n\times n} by  
       \Phi _W'(t)=(G(t)-W(t))\Phi _W(t), \Phi _W(a)=I_n.  (\dagger )

    Show that for every t\in [a,a+\delta )  
       V(t)= sup_{W\in L^2_loc} \Xi (t,W),                  (\ddagger )  
    where  
       \Xi (t,W)=\Phi _W(t)^{-^T} S_0 \Phi _W(t)^{-1}  
               -\int _a^{t} \Phi _W(t)^{-^T} \Phi _W(s)^T[W(s)^2-Q(s)]\Phi _W(s) \Phi _W(t)^{-1} ds.  

    (The supremum is taken with respect to the Loewner order on S^n; it is actually a maximum and is attained for the choice W=V.)

    You may assume \delta  is chosen so that V is finite on [a,a+\delta ).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Part 1: Linear comparison in the Loewner order  
---------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  Fundamental matrix.  
Solve the auxiliary linear system  
   \Psi '(t)=G(t)\Psi (t), \Psi (a)=I_n.  
Local integrability of G guarantees an absolutely continuous, point-wise invertible \Psi  with \Psi ,\Psi ^{-1} locally absolutely continuous.

Step 2.  Conjugation.  
Put X(t):=\Psi (t)^T V(t) \Psi (t).  Because V and \Psi  are absolutely continuous, so is X.  Differentiating,

   X'(t)=\Psi (t)^T[ V'(t)+G(t)^TV(t)+V(t)G(t) ]\Psi (t).

Using the hypothesis V'+G^TV+VG \succeq  H,

   X'(t) \succeq  \Psi (t)^T H(t) \Psi (t).               (1)

Step 3.  Comparison with the linear solution.  
Define X_U(t):=\Psi (t)^T U(t) \Psi (t); since U satisfies the equality,

   X_U'(t)=\Psi (t)^T H(t) \Psi (t).               (2)

Set Y(t):=X(t)-X_U(t).  From (1)-(2) we have Y'(t) \succeq  0 a.e. and Y(a)=0, hence Y(t) \succeq  0 for all t\geq a.  Multiplying by \Psi (t)^{-^T} (left) and \Psi (t)^{-1} (right) preserves the Loewner order, giving V(t) \succeq  U(t).

Part 2: Variational representation for the Riccati solution  
----------------------------------------------------------------
Throughout let W\in L^2_loc([a,a+\delta );S^n) be arbitrary.

Step 1.  A quadratic matrix inequality.  
For symmetric matrices Z,W the square (Z-W)^2 is positive-semidefinite; expanding,

   (Z-W)^2 = Z^2 - ZW - WZ + W^2 \succeq  0  
   \Rightarrow  Z^2 \succeq  ZW + WZ - W^2.               (3)

Taking Z=V(t) (symmetric) in (3) we obtain

   V(t)^2 \succeq  V(t)W(t) + W(t)V(t) - W(t)^2.        (4)

Step 2.  Inserting (4) into the Riccati equation.  
Equation (\star ) and (4) give, for a.e. t,

   V' + G^TV + VG 
     = V^2 + Q
     \succeq  V W + W V - W^2 + Q.             (5)

Re-grouping the mixed terms,

   V' + (G-W)^TV + V(G-W) \succeq  Q - W^2.        (6)

Notice that (G-W)^TV+V(G-W) is symmetric because V and W are.

Step 3.  Conjugation by the flow \Phi _W.  
Let \Phi _W solve (\dagger ) and set  

   X_W(t):=\Phi _W(t)^T V(t) \Phi _W(t).           (7)

Because \Phi _W'=(G-W)\Phi _W and \Phi _W is invertible, differentiating (7) yields

   X_W'(t)=\Phi _W(t)^T
            [ V' + (G-W)^TV + V(G-W) ](t)
            \Phi _W(t)
          \succeq  \Phi _W(t)^T[ Q(t) - W(t)^2 ] \Phi _W(t).   (8)

Step 4.  Integration.  
Integrate (8) from a to t (\Phi _W(a)=I_n):

   X_W(t) \succeq  S_0 - \int _a^{t} \Phi _W(s)^T[ W(s)^2 - Q(s) ] \Phi _W(s) ds. (9)

Step 5.  Returning to V.  
Premultiplying (9) by \Phi _W(t)^{-^T} and post-multiplying by \Phi _W(t)^{-1} gives

   V(t) \succeq  \Phi _W(t)^{-^T}S_0\Phi _W(t)^{-1}
          -\int _a^{t} \Phi _W(t)^{-^T} \Phi _W(s)^T[ W(s)^2-Q(s) ]
                     \Phi _W(s) \Phi _W(t)^{-1} ds
        = \Xi (t,W).                 (10)

Hence V(t) dominates every \Xi (t,W); therefore

   V(t) \succeq  sup_{W\in L^2_loc} \Xi (t,W).          (11)

Step 6.  Equality for the maximising choice W=V.  
Choose W=V.  In (3) we then have equality, and consequently every ``\succeq '' in (5)-(10) becomes ``=''.  Thus \Xi (t,V)=V(t), so V(t) realises the supremum.  Therefore the supremum in (\ddagger ) is a maximum attained at W=V and (11) holds with equality:

   V(t)= sup_{W\in L^2_loc} \Xi (t,W)=\Xi (t,V).  

Step 7.  Well-posedness remarks.  
* For each W the flow \Phi _W exists and is invertible because G-W is locally integrable.  
* The integrand in \Xi (t,W) is locally square-integrable, hence the integral is finite on [a,t).  
* Although (S^n,\succeq ) is not a lattice when n\geq 2, the supremum in (\ddagger ) exists since it is achieved at W=V.

This completes the proof of (\ddagger ).

• Higher dimensional structure: The scalar functions of the original problem are replaced by symmetric n×n matrices, forcing the use of the non-trivial Loewner partial order and properties of matrix positive–semidefiniteness.

• Riccati dynamics: The non-linear part is now matrix-quadratic (V²), the natural multivariate analogue of the scalar v², and requires careful handling via the inequality (Z−W)² ≽ 0.  
• Control–theoretic flavour: The representation involves an auxiliary matrix control W and a matrix differential equation (†) for Φ_W, mirroring advanced linear–quadratic optimal–control theory.
